_comment: Portfolio blog & experience entries. Add new items to the entries array below. No HTML changes needed — the UI updates automatically.

_schema.title: string — required
_schema.tag: string — label shown on the card (e.g. Internship, Blog Post, Project)
_schema.summary: string — 1–2 sentence preview shown on the list card
_schema.content: array of strings — each string becomes a paragraph in the modal
_schema.image: string — path or URL to image (optional); falls back to emoji
_schema.emoji: string — fallback emoji if no image (optional)
_schema.link: string — URL to external project or article (optional)

entries.title: Data Analytics Intern @ FinTrack Ltd
entries.summary: Spent 12 weeks building automated reporting pipelines and a Tableau dashboard that reduced manual finance reporting time by 60%.
content: Over the summer of 2024, I joined FinTrack Ltd — a Edinburgh-based fintech startup — as a Data Analytics Intern. The team was small (6 analysts) which meant I got significant ownership of real projects from day one.

My main project was redesigning the weekly finance reporting pipeline. The existing process involved manually running 14 SQL queries, copying results into Excel, and emailing PDFs to stakeholders. I automated this end-to-end using Python (pandas + SQLAlchemy) and scheduled it with cron, cutting the time from 3 hours to 8 minutes per week.

I also built a Tableau dashboard for the CFO's team to track ARR, churn rate, and LTV by customer segment. This replaced a static Excel sheet and enabled the finance team to explore data interactively for the first time.

Key lessons: Always validate your data pipelines with real stakeholders early; the first version of the dashboard had metrics defined differently than the finance team expected. Communication matters as much as technical skill.

entries.title: Building a Football Analytics Model with StatsBomb Data
entries.summary: How I used open StatsBomb event data to build an expected-goals (xG) model and visualise pass networks for EPL matches.
content: Football analytics has exploded in the past decade, and StatsBomb's decision to release free open data was a game-changer for hobbyists like me. In this post I'll walk through how I built a basic xG model using their open dataset.

The data contains over 3,000 matches worth of event-level data — every pass, shot, dribble, and defensive action tagged with x/y coordinates and contextual metadata. I focused on shot events to build my xG model.

My feature set included: distance to goal, angle to goal, shot type (header/foot), preceding action type (cross/through ball/open play), and game state (score difference at time of shot). I trained a Logistic Regression and an XGBoost classifier, with XGBoost achieving a log-loss of 0.48 on the held-out test set.

The most interesting part was visualising pass networks per team per match. By aggregating pass start/end coordinates and weighting edges by pass frequency, you can see how different managers implement their tactical philosophy — Barcelona's tiki-taka vs Liverpool's high press look completely different on a pass network graph.
Full code and notebook available on GitHub. Next steps: incorporating tracking data if I can get access, and experimenting with spatial smoothing for the shot coordinate features.

entries.title: My Journey Learning SQL: From SELECT * to Window Functions
entries.summary: A structured guide to how I went from writing basic queries to complex analytical SQL in 6 months — resources, mistakes, and lessons learned.
content: When I started my CS degree, SQL felt like a niche skill — something database administrators used, not 'real' developers. Two internship applications later, where SQL was listed as a key requirement for every single data role I applied to, I changed my mind fast.

Month 1-2: I started with Mode Analytics' SQL School and SQLZoo. I focused on the basics: SELECT, WHERE, GROUP BY, JOINs (all four types). The classic mistake everyone makes is confusing LEFT JOIN with INNER JOIN behaviour — I made it at least a dozen times before it clicked.

Month 3-4: Subqueries, CTEs, and CASE statements. CTEs (Common Table Expressions) were a revelation — they made complex queries readable. I started writing every multi-step query as a CTE chain, even when it wasn't strictly necessary, just to practice the pattern.

Month 5-6: Window functions. These are the single most powerful tool in analytical SQL and the most underused by beginners. ROW_NUMBER(), RANK(), LAG(), LEAD(), and running totals with SUM() OVER (PARTITION BY ... ORDER BY ...) covered 90% of what I needed in my internship.

Best resource I found: the 'Serious SQL' course by Danny Ma. Expensive but worth it — he builds realistic business problems rather than toy examples. Highly recommend pairing it with LeetCode's SQL problems (Easy → Medium is a good range for interview prep).

entries.title: University Data Science Society — Project Lead
entries.summary: Led a team of 8 students to build a sentiment analysis tool for Scottish Parliament debates, presented at the Edinburgh Data Science Symposium.
content: In my second year I joined the University of Edinburgh Data Science Society and within a semester was leading the 'Civic Tech' project track — a group of 8 students building data tools with real-world impact.

Our project: analysing Scottish Parliament Official Reports (Hansard-style transcripts) to track sentiment trends over time, identify the most discussed topics by party, and build a searchable dashboard. We wanted to make democratic debate more accessible to the public.

Technical stack: Python for scraping (BeautifulSoup + requests), spaCy for NLP preprocessing, VADER for sentiment analysis (fast and surprisingly good for formal speech), and Plotly Dash for the dashboard. We hosted on a free Render instance.

The project was accepted to present at the Edinburgh Data Science Symposium in March 2024 — my first experience presenting research to a professional audience. The Q&A was tough but incredible for my confidence.

Running a student team taught me as much about project management as it did about data science. Weekly stand-ups, GitHub project boards, and clear deliverable ownership made the difference between the project shipping and dying in group-chat limbo.

entries.title: Why Every CS Student Should Learn Tableau (Even If You're Not a Data Person)
entries.summary: Tableau took me 48 hours to get productive with and landed me my first internship. Here's why I think it's the highest ROI tool for CS students to learn.
content: Controversial opinion: Tableau is more immediately useful for most CS students than React, Docker, or Kubernetes. I'll explain why.

We're trained to think in code. When a non-technical stakeholder needs to understand data, our instinct is to write a script, generate a static chart, and email a PNG. The problem: they immediately ask for the chart sliced a different way, or filtered by a different date range. Then you're writing another script. And another.

Tableau (and Power BI, if you're in a Microsoft shop) solves this by putting the slice-and-dice power in the hands of the person asking the questions. You build the data model and the dashboard once; they explore it themselves.

I spent 48 hours with Tableau Public over one exam-free week in first year. I built a dashboard analysing UK rental prices using ONS data. That dashboard became the portfolio piece that got me my first internship interview — and eventually the offer.

Free alternatives that are almost as good: Power BI Desktop (Windows only, free), Google Looker Studio (browser-based, free), and Metabase (self-hosted). If you're on Linux, Metabase is my recommendation. The core concepts transfer between all of them.
